name: Mezzanine layout
description: Floor-layout strategy that inserts a small intermediate gallery floor partway up a tall single-volume space, covering only 20-40% of the footprint so the double-height void below remains the dominant room. The mezzanine reads as a balcony or loft hanging over the main hall rather than a true second storey: it is reached by a single staircase or spiral, edged by a continuous railing, and serves a quiet secondary use — a library second tier, a theater balcony, a home office or reading lounge above a modern living room. Because the deck does not span the full footprint, light and sound still travel up through the open portion, keeping the two levels perceptually one room.
placement_rules: Restrict the mezzanine deck to 20-40% of the ground-floor footprint; above 40% it stops reading as a gallery and becomes a half-floor, below 20% it cannot host useful furniture.
Anchor the deck against one or two walls (typically a back wall or an L corner) and leave the long open edge facing the tallest part of the void below so the balcony overlooks the main space.
Hold the mezzanine soffit 4 to 6 blocks above the ground floor: 4 blocks is the minimum that still feels generous beneath, 6 blocks lets a tall living-room volume read as monumental.
Provide exactly one access point — a straight or quarter-turn staircase, or a spiral stair in tight footprints — landing on the deck against a wall, never in the middle of the gallery.
Edge the entire open perimeter with a continuous railing or low parapet (1-block tall minimum) — this is not optional, the deck must read as safely enclosed from the void.
Keep the upstairs use quiet and visual: bookshelves and a reading chair, a desk facing the railing, a small lounge — never plumbing, never the bed, never a kitchen.
Align the deck so at least one tall window on the double-height wall passes the mezzanine at eye level, giving the gallery its own framed view rather than just borrowing light from below.
